title: Electric-Field Control of Quantum Tunneling Regimes in Focused He-Ion-Beam-Irradiated Oxide Interfaces
authors: Yu Chen, Maria D'Antuono, Robin Hutt, Cesar Magen, Edward Goldobin, Dieter Koelle, Reinhold Kleiner, Marco Salluzzo, Daniela Stornaiuolo
abstract: Helium focused ion beam irradiation enables the fabrication of tunnel field-effect transistors based on two-dimensional electron systems (2DESs) at an oxide interface.High resolution scanning transmission electron microscopy and strain mapping reveal localized lattice deformation confined to the irradiated regions, which act as nanoscale potential barriers. The barrier profile can be continuously tuned by electrostatic backgating at low temperature without degrading the electronic properties of the 2DES electrodes. Transport measurements demonstrate controlled access to thermionic emission, direct tunneling, and Fowler-Nordheim tunneling within a single device architecture. These results establish He FIB irradiation as a powerful tool for nanoscale functional engineering of complex-oxide interfaces and provide a platform for exploring gate-tunable quantum tunneling phenomena.
summary: This paper demonstrates a new way to engineer nanoscale barriers in oxide interfaces using a Helium ion beam. By inducing local lattice strain, the researchers created tunnel field-effect transistors where the conduction mechanism can be switched between thermionic emission and different tunneling regimes using an electric field. This provides a powerful tool for creating tunable quantum nanodevices in complex oxide heterostructures.
main_problem: The difficulty of fabricating planar tunnel field-effect transistors (TFETs) at buried oxide interfaces and the need for a method to create tunable, nanoscale potential barriers without degrading the 2D electron system.
main_result: He-FIB irradiation enables the creation of nanoscale potential barriers via localized lattice strain, allowing a single device to continuously tune between thermionic emission, direct tunneling, and Fowler-Nordheim tunneling via backgating.
method: The researchers used Helium Focused Ion Beam (He-FIB) irradiation to pattern the LaAlO3/SrTiO3 interface, followed by high-resolution STEM for structural analysis and low-temperature transport measurements.
model_or_system: The experimental platform consists of LaAlO3/SrTiO3 (LAO/STO) heterostructures hosting a two-dimensional electron system (2DES), where He-FIB-induced strain creates nanoscale potential barriers.
key_observables: Threshold voltage (Vth), barrier height (Phi_B), barrier width (d), in-plane lattice strain (epsilon_xx), and current-voltage (I-V) characteristics.
important_parameters: Transport regimes (thermionic emission, direct tunneling, Fowler-Nordheim tunneling), irradiation dose, and back-gate voltage (Vbg).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the Richardson-Schottky model for thermionic emission and specific geometric profiles (trapezoidal/triangular) for tunneling regimes.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows STEM images and strain maps of irradiated regions; Fig 2 displays device layout, I-V curves, and temporal evolution of Vth; Fig 3 shows gate-dependent I-V curves at 5K; Fig S1-S3 detail temporal stability and high-temperature transport analysis.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the fabrication challenge, describes the He-FIB method and structural characterization (STEM/strain), presents transport measurements across different tunneling regimes, discusses the tunability via backgating, and concludes with the temporal stability of the induced barriers.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: (a,c) Cross-sectional STEM images of LAO/STO heterostructures irradiated with He-FIB at doses of 320ions/nm (a) and 160ions/nm (c). A fracture extending into the STO substrate is observed at the higher dose, while no clear discontinuity is visible at the lower dose. (b,d). Corresponding in-plane strain maps (ϵxx). The unstrained substrate lattice parameter, far from the irradiated area, is used as a reference. (e) In- plane strain profiles along the white arrows in (b) and (d), vertically integrated along the dashed rectangles.

Figure 2: (a) Sketch of the devices layout and measurement geometry for device 2. (b) I-V curve of device A2 (dose: 50ions/nm) measured at 5 K shortly after He-FIB irradiation. Vth is defined as the voltage-axis intercept of a linear fit to the high-current region (red line). In the inset we show the I-V of the same device measured 6 months after irradiation. (c) Evolution of Vth as a function of the time for different doses.

Figure 3: I − V curves of (a) device B1 (dose: 250ions/nm) measured at t3, (b) device B2 (dose: 500ions/nm) measured at t2 and (c) device B3 (dose: 1000ions/nm) measured at t2 for different values of the increasing back-gate voltage (arrow) (see also Fig. S2 in the Supporting Information). All the measurements were performed at T = 5 K.

Figure 4: Sketches of three possible tunnel mechanisms: thermal activation (a), direct tunneling (b), and Fowler-Nordhaim tunneling (c). Panels (d), (e), (f) show the data of Fig. 3(a), (b), (c) respectively, plot as ln(J/V2) vs. (1/V). The arrows indicate the direction of increasing Vbg. The gray dashed line is a guide to the eye for the inflection point Vi, signaling the transition to F-N tunneling at high V. the barrier width d from the F–N fits to the low-temperature data. (iii) Finally, extracted d is used to quantify gate-induced modulation of ΦB in both the F–N and direct tunneling regimes. Figure 5: Barrier energy ΦB as a function of Vth calculated from thermal activation (black squares) and using the F-N fit of the curves shown in Fig. 4: blue, purple and dark red triangles corresponds to the data shown in panels (d), (e) and (f) respectively.
